event: Hominins inhabit the Horn of Africa during the Stone Age, leaving stone tools across what is now Somalia.
Prehistoric peoples create the rock art at Laas Geel near Hargeisa, among the earliest known in the Horn of Africa.
The region forms part of the Land of Punt, a trading partner of ancient Egypt prized for incense, myrrh, and gold.
Egyptian expeditions under pharaohs such as Hatshepsut conduct extensive maritime trade with the Land of Punt.
Somali coastal ports such as Opone trade frankincense, myrrh, and spices with Egypt, Greece, Rome, and India.
The Periplus of the Erythraean Sea describes flourishing Somali trading towns along the northern coast.
Arab and Persian merchants establish settlements, and Islam begins spreading along the Somali coast.
The port city of Mogadishu emerges as a major Indian Ocean trading hub.
The Sultanate of Mogadishu rises, minting its own coinage and dominating East African gold trade.
The Ajuran Sultanate begins consolidating power across southern Somalia and the Horn of Africa.
Moroccan traveler Ibn Battuta visits Mogadishu and describes it as a large, prosperous, and well-organized city.
Chinese admiral Zheng He's fleet reaches Somali ports, including Mogadishu and Barawa, during its Indian Ocean voyages.
Portuguese ships under Vasco da Gama begin probing the Somali coast, opening conflict over Indian Ocean trade.
Imam Ahmad ibn Ibrahim al-Ghazi (Gurey) launches his conquest of Abyssinia from the Adal Sultanate.
The Ajuran Sultanate repels Portuguese incursions and maintains control of key southern ports.
The Ajuran Sultanate declines as rival dynasties and the Geledi Sultanate rise in the interior.
The Geledi Sultanate consolidates power over the Shebelle River region of southern Somalia.
Britain establishes a coaling station at Aden, increasing strategic interest in the opposite Somali coast.
The opening of the Suez Canal heightens European competition for control of the Horn of Africa.
Britain begins signing protectorate treaties with northern Somali clans, forming British Somaliland.
Italy establishes protectorates in the south and northeast, forming the basis of Italian Somaliland.
Sayyid Mohammed Abdullah Hassan launches the Dervish movement, a two-decade resistance against colonial rule.
British aerial bombardment crushes the Dervish state, ending Sayyid Mohammed Abdullah Hassan's resistance.
Italian Somaliland is incorporated into Italian East Africa after Italy's conquest of Ethiopia.
British forces defeat the Italians and take control of both British and Italian Somaliland during World War II.
Italian Somaliland becomes a UN Trust Territory administered by Italy with a ten-year path to independence.
British Somaliland and Italian Somaliland unite on July 1 to form the independent Somali Republic.
Aden Abdullah Osman Daar becomes the first President of independent Somalia.
Somalia joins the Organisation of African Unity at its founding and pursues Greater Somalia ambitions.
Mohamed Siad Barre seizes power in a military coup after the assassination of President Abdirashid Ali Shermarke.
Siad Barre's government adopts a Latin-based script for the Somali language, boosting mass literacy.
Somalia joins the Arab League and signs a friendship treaty with the Soviet Union.
Somalia invades Ethiopia's Ogaden region, triggering the Ogaden War.
Somali forces are defeated in the Ogaden War after the USSR and Cuba back Ethiopia, shifting Somalia toward the West.
Government forces bomb Hargeisa during a brutal crackdown on the Somali National Movement, killing tens of thousands.
Siad Barre is overthrown, plunging Somalia into civil war and state collapse.
The northwest declares independence as the unrecognized Republic of Somaliland.
Famine and warlord conflict prompt the UN-authorized, US-led Operation Restore Hope humanitarian intervention.
The Battle of Mogadishu (Black Hawk Down) kills 18 US soldiers, hastening Western withdrawal.
UN peacekeepers withdraw from Somalia, leaving the country fragmented among rival warlords.
The northeastern region declares itself the autonomous state of Puntland within a future federal Somalia.
A Transitional Federal Government is formed in Kenya to attempt national reconciliation.
The Islamic Courts Union seizes Mogadishu before Ethiopian forces intervene to oust it.
The African Union deploys the AMISOM peacekeeping mission to support the transitional government.
Somali piracy off the Horn of Africa peaks, drawing international naval patrols to the Gulf of Aden.
A devastating famine kills an estimated quarter-million people as al-Shabaab restricts aid access.
A new Federal Government of Somalia is established with a provisional constitution, ending the transitional period.
A truck bombing in Mogadishu kills over 500 people in one of the deadliest terrorist attacks in modern history.
Hassan Sheikh Mohamud is elected president and launches a major military offensive against al-Shabaab.
Somalia takes a non-permanent seat on the UN Security Council and joins the East African Community.
